hook: You already know how to count — but what happens when the objects you're counting are arrangements, selections, or overlapping sets that make naive counting hopelessly error-prone? A single missed case or double-counted outcome can collapse an entire argument. This unit hands you a precise toolkit so that 'how many ways?' always has a clean, confident answer.
concept: Combinatorics is the art and science of counting structured collections without listing them all — the unifying idea is that clever organization and symmetry let us replace exhaustive enumeration with elegant formulas, captured formally through the study of enumeration and generating functions.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to decompose complex counting problems into sequential or alternative choices, applying the product and sum rules to compute totals quickly and correctly.
Master the distinction between ordered arrangements ($P(n,k)$) and unordered selections ($C(n,k)$), and apply the right formula depending on whether order matters in a given scenario.
Discover how the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ governs the expansion of $(x+y)^n$ and how Pascal's Triangle encodes recursive relationships you can exploit in proofs and calculations.
Apply the deceptively simple idea that if $n+1$ objects occupy $n$ containers then some container holds at least two, to prove surprising existence results in mathematics and computer science.
Use the inclusion-exclusion formula $|A \cup B \cup C| = |A|+|B|+|C|-|A\cap B|-\cdots+|A\cap B\cap C|$ to count elements in overlapping sets by carefully adding and subtracting intersections.
Learn to encode a sequence of counts as the coefficients of a power series, then manipulate that series algebraically to solve counting problems that resist direct methods.
Translate combinatorial processes into recurrence relations like $a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2}$ and solve them using characteristic roots or generating functions to find closed-form expressions.

Explore how the Catalan numbers $C_n = \frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$ count diverse structures — from valid bracket sequences to binary trees — and prove powerful combinatorial identities that unify the unit's themes.
